result.chapter_summary: Hirsch argues that the concept of 'general reading-comprehension skills' is a delusion, asserting instead that reading comprehension depends entirely on the application of domain-specific background knowledge. He synthesizes research on short-term memory limits and the 'baseball study' to demonstrate that knowledge is the primary determinant of literacy, rather than transferable skills.

claims.text: The claim made by schools that they are teaching general 'reading-comprehension skills' is a delusion.
claims.source_passage: Current science explains why the claim made by our schools that they are teaching general “reading-comprehension skills” is a delusion.

claims.text: The notion of general reading skills was put in place to defend a disorganized or 'helter-skelter' curriculum.
claims.source_passage: It’s a notion that was put in place to defend a helter-skelter curriculum that often had to be decided by the classroom teachers themselves.

claims.text: There are no general reading comprehension skills like 'finding the main idea.'
claims.source_passage: Since there are no general reading comprehension skills like “finding the main idea,” once a person learns to sound out the words, the entire skill of reading depends on applying the right background knowledge.

claims.text: Once a person learns to decode words, the entire skill of reading depends on applying the right background knowledge.
claims.source_passage: once a person learns to sound out the words, the entire skill of reading depends on applying the right background knowledge.

claims.text: Background knowledge enables a reader to disambiguate and amplify the meanings of written words.
claims.source_passage: That alone enables a person to disambiguate and amplify the meanings of the written words.

claims.text: Successful communication requires that the writer and reader share the same relevant background knowledge to infer what is implied.
claims.source_passage: Hence the writer and the reader, speaker and listener, must share the same relevant background knowledge to infer (carry from) what had been implied in (folded into) the words.

claims.text: Knowledge of a content domain is a powerful enough determinant of information recall to allow poor readers to compensate for low reading ability.
claims.source_passage: It appeared to teachers therefore that knowledge of a content domain is a powerful determinant of the amount and quality of information recalled, powerful enough for poor readers to compensate for their generally low reading ability.

claims.text: 'Generally low reading ability' (given adequate decoding) is simply a lack of the national-ethnic background knowledge shared by the 'good readers' in a society.
claims.source_passage: But what is “generally low reading ability,” given adequate decoding? It simply means low national-ethnic background knowledge shared by the good readers in a society.

claims.text: Scientific research has refuted the model that reading follows an orderly pattern from words to sentences to whole text meanings.
claims.source_passage: new research refuted the old thinking that reading follows an orderly pattern. We don’t first identify words, then word meanings, then combine word meanings to get the meanings of sentences, and finally combine sentence meanings to get the meaning of a whole text.

claims.text: The reader is a supplier of essential cultural information that is not written down in the text.
claims.source_passage: It brings to the fore the highly active mind of the reader, who is now discovered to be not only a decoder of what is written down, but also a supplier of much essential cultural information that is not written down.

claims.text: The larger part of a text's meaning lies below the surface and is composed of the reader's relevant knowledge.
claims.source_passage: The explicit meanings of a piece of writing are the tip of an iceberg of meaning; the larger part lies below the surface of the text and is composed of the reader’s own relevant knowledge about the subject

claims.text: Short-term memory is limited to remembering between four and seven discrete items presented once without rehearsal.
claims.source_passage: ly remember more than four to seven discrete items that have been presented once without rehearsal.

claims.text: Chunking items into groupings is a device used to resist the built-in limitations of short-term memory.
claims.source_passage: One device that memory experts have developed to resist this built-in limitation is “chunking.” We can remember telephone numbers and other series of discrete items if we chunk the disparate items into a smaller number of groupings.

claims.text: Failure to perform tasks reliably occurs whenever a task consistently forces the brain to exceed the capacity of short-term memory.
claims.source_passage: Whenever we try to perform a task that consistently forces us to exceed the capacity of short-term memory, we fail to perform it reliably.

claims.text: The capacity limit of short-term memory is hardwired into the human system and applies to experts as well as ordinary people.
claims.source_passage: That is a limit on memory experts, as well as on ordinary people. Memory experts have worked out indirect tricks to circumvent this universal limitation, but they can never directly overcome it, because the constraint is hardwired into our systems.

claims.text: Chunking allows the mind to remember longer series of discrete items by grouping them into a smaller number of units.
claims.source_passage: One device that memory experts have developed to resist this built-in limitation is “chunking.” We can remember telephone numbers and other series of discrete items if we chunk the disparate items into a smaller number of groupings.

claims.text: Short-term memory functions as a temporary vestibule where incoming items are given structure before being transferred to long-term memory.
claims.source_passage: Short-term memory is the mind’s vestibule where incoming items enjoy a brief equality lasting just long enough for the mind to give them a structure.

claims.text: The limitation of short-term memory necessitates that all human languages divide sentences into brief, semi-complete clausal units.
claims.source_passage: The limitation imposed by short-term memory accounts for the fact that all languages must form brief bursts of words into clauses. Every known language divides its sentences into semi-complete clausal units that are small enough to be structured within the limitations of short-term memory.

claims.text: The clause is the primary perceptual unit in language processing.
claims.source_passage: As the psycholinguist T. G. Bever explains in three steps : “(1) The clause is the primary perceptual unit; (2) within each clause we assign semantic relations within major phrases; (3) after each clause is processed, it is recoded into relatively abstract form”

claims.text: Language is transferred to long-term memory as a shorthand recoding of the 'gist' rather than a literal recollection of words.
claims.source_passage: Bever says that language is transferred from short-term memory into long-term memory not as a literal recollection of words, but as a shorthand recoding of their gist, which normally erases from memory many of the individual words.

claims.text: Human memory for the literal words of a sentence is poor, whereas long-term memory for the gist of those sentences is reliable.
claims.source_passage: On the basis of this account, we could predict that our memories for the literal words that make up sentences should be poor, whereas our long-term memories for their gist should be quite reliable. That is indeed the case

claims.text: The loss of literal surface form in favor of meaning is an evolutionary advantage that prevents the mind's 'hard disk' from filling with clutter.
claims.source_passage: From an evolutionary standpoint it is much more desirable that we forget the literal words we encounter and remember their meanings than that we have an absolute memory for both words and meanings. If we remembered all the surface details of our experiences... the hard disks in our minds would quickly fill to capacity

claims.text: Surface forms of sentences are lost to human memory within a few seconds of processing.
claims.source_passage: In fact, experimenters have found that surface forms of sentences are lost to memory within a few seconds.

claims.text: The original surface form of a sentence is rapidly lost to memory, whereas an accurate memory for its meaning is retained.
claims.source_passage: Sachs inferred from these results, and later experimentation has amply confirmed her inference, that the original form of a sentence is rapidly lost to memory, whereas an accurate memory for its meaning is retained.

claims.text: Language users often fail to notice when a synonym has been substituted in a test sentence, provided the substitution preserves the meaning.
claims.source_passage: In 1966, S. Fillenbaum showed that subjects don’t notice that a synonym has been substituted in a test sentence.

claims.text: Linguistic inferences regarding synonyms and implications are made at the moment of understanding rather than during the later process of recall.
claims.source_passage: Most remarkable of all, it was discovered about a decade later that inferences regarding open versus not closed and short versus not tall are made at the time a sentence is understood, not later in recognition or recall.

claims.text: Accurate memory of the exact wording of a sentence can disappear in as little time as it takes to speak sixteen syllables.
claims.source_passage: Eric Wanner showed that listeners had already lost the exact wording of a sentence when only sixteen syllables intervened between the sentence and the test.

claims.text: The initial understanding of any text depends on the reader or listener applying relevant background knowledge that is not explicitly provided in the text.
claims.source_passage: In a series of experiments, they found that our initial understanding of a text depends on our applying relevant background knowledge that is not given in the text itself.

claims.text: Language comprehension involves a constructive process where we go beyond literal meanings to supply unstated implications.
claims.source_passage: Under this second theory, we always go beyond a text’s literal meanings to supply important implications that were not explicitly stated by the words of the text.

claims.text: Human beings store a full mental model of a text's implications in long-term memory rather than just the literal meanings of the words.
claims.source_passage: We then store the fuller model of the text’s meaning in long-term memory. This constructive hypothesis, in other words, proved to be correct.

claims.text: Human memory does not store the literal words of a sentence but rather an elaborate model of what the words imply.
claims.source_passage: Under this second theory, we always go beyond a text’s literal meanings to supply important implications that were not explicitly stated by the words of the text. We then store the fuller model of the text’s meaning in long-term memory.

claims.text: Inferences based on prior knowledge are part of verbal meaning from the very beginning of the comprehension process.
claims.source_passage: The answer given by this, and other experiments is “Yes, that is the nature of verbal meaning-inferences based on prior knowledge are part of meaning from the very beginning.”

claims.text: Mental models based on prior knowledge and the words of a text exert mutual influences on each other during the reading process.
claims.source_passage: From this finding we can predict that there will be mutual influences between the mental models we have in our minds on the basis of prior knowledge along with the words of the text as we read them.

claims.text: An integrated mental model is essential to the understanding and remembering of discourse.
claims.source_passage: Apparently, such an integrated model is essential to understanding and remembering discourse.

claims.text: Informationally deprived people can often decode individual sentences but cannot make sense of a whole text due to missing referential clues.
claims.source_passage: Informationally deprived people constantly run across passages that look like the Bransford one, because the texts contain important referential clues they can’t understand. Although they can read the individual sentences, they can’t make sense out of the whole.

claims.text: Differences in reading ability between five-year-olds and eight-year-olds are caused primarily by the older children’s possessing more knowledge, rather than differences in memory capacities, reasoning abilities, or control of eye movements.
claims.source_passage: T. Trabasso and his colleagues discovered that differences in reading ability between five-year-olds and eight-year-olds are caused primarily by the older children’s possessing more knowledge, not by differences in their memory capacities, reasoning abilities, or control of eye movements.

claims.text: Among children with identical IQ and general reading scores, those with specific prior knowledge of a topic show superior reading skills when reading about that topic.
claims.source_passage: Similar work by P. D. Pearson and his associates has shown that, among seven-year-olds who score the same on reading and IQ tests, those who have greater knowledge relevant to the text at hand show superior reading skills.

claims.text: Specific topic knowledge improves reading comprehension even when the text contains no special concepts or difficult words.
claims.source_passage: Those who knew a little about them were much better readers of a story involving spiders than were the other children, even though the story contained no special concepts or difficult words.

claims.text: Educators have failed to acknowledge or implement the practical implications of research proving the role of background knowledge in reading comprehension.
claims.source_passage: One might be tempted to say that these results are predictable and obvious. Nevertheless, educators have not acknowledged or acted on the implications of such results.

claims.text: The reading process is constrained by a narrow window of attention dictated by the limits of short-term memory.
claims.source_passage: the reader is confined to a rather narrow window of attention that is limited by short-term memory.

claims.text: Past knowledge is the mechanism used to interpret momentary fragments of experience and place them within larger functional wholes.
claims.source_passage: We use past knowledge to interpret this window of experience, to place its momentary fragments within larger wholes that give them a function and a place.

claims.text: Linguistic perception remains meaningless unless fragments are placed within larger, non-visible wholes based on past knowledge.
claims.source_passage: The raw data of visual or linguistic perception come in bits and pieces, and they will remain meaningless if they continue to be nothing but colored flecks or mere sequences of letters or words.

claims.text: Human beings naturally interpret their experiences through middle categories that are neither too specific nor too general.
claims.source_passage: if no other constraints are at work, people tend to interpret their experiences through middle categories, that is, through classifications that are neither specific, like oak leaves, nor general, like flora.

claims.text: Middle-level categories serve as the most efficient instruments for perceiving the world and acting in it.
claims.source_passage: We normally interpret experience through the categories that have been most useful to us in the past. We try them out initially as the most efficient instruments for perceiving the world and acting in it.

claims.text: Middle-level categories are the first classifications children learn during language acquisition.
claims.source_passage: Research has shown that middle-level categories are the ones children learn first in acquiring language; they learn tree before oak, and they learn dog before animal.

claims.text: Middle-level categories constitute the primary conceptual structure of the human world.
claims.source_passage: In a number of papers she has argued that middle-level categories are the basic furniture of our conceptual world.

claims.text: In the absence of specific context, people interpret the world through basic classifications and commonly encountered examples.
claims.source_passage: She has demonstrated that, in the absence of any special context or constraint, people understand the world in terms of basic classifications. Furthermore, people understand these normal classifications through commonly encountered examples.

claims.text: People within a shared culture tend to agree on which members of a category are the most 'typical' examples.
claims.source_passage: If people in our culture tend to agree that a robin is more typical than a pheasant in the bird category, it is likely that, other things being equal, they will think of a robin-sort-of-creature when they hear the word bird.

claims.text: Sentences that sound reasonable with a general class name often become absurd when an untypical member of that class is substituted.
claims.source_passage: In isolated sentences, when an untypical member of a class is substituted for the class name, sentences that previously sounded reasonable often fall into absurdity.

claims.text: Shared ethnicity and literacy are defined by agreement on typical examples of a class.
claims.source_passage: People with the same ethnicity and literacy agree about typical examples of a class and the isolated sentences they make with class names work best with typical examples.

claims.text: Mental prototypes of category words constitute the 'usual furniture of our minds' and are used daily in perception and communication.
claims.source_passage: This finding suggests that we hold in our minds typical exemplars or prototypes of the category words we use and that these prototypes constitute the usual furniture of our minds.

claims.text: Category prototypes are processed much faster in the brain than atypical examples when verifying truth or falsity.
claims.source_passage: Rosch and others have shown that such prototypes are processed much faster than atypical examples in verifying the truth or falsity of isolated sentences.

claims.text: Mental models or prototypes are essential for how people within a specific ethnicity apply past knowledge to comprehend speech.
claims.source_passage: Knowing about prototypes is essential for understanding how the people within a given ethnicity apply past knowledge to the comprehension of speech.

claims.text: Meaning is created by assimilating present experiences into prototypes formed from past experiences.
claims.source_passage: We are able to make our present experiences take on meaning by assimilating them to prototypes formed from our past experiences.

claims.text: Schemata are abstract mental entities rather than concrete images.
claims.source_passage: Schema and its plural schemata correctly suggest somewhat abstract mental entities rather than concrete images.

claims.text: Mental schemata for categories like 'bird' differ significantly between cultures, such as between America and Australia.
claims.source_passage: If Rosch had tested her subjects in Australia, her results would probably have been different, and she would have found the typical bird schema to be unlike a robin in many respects.

claims.text: Initial schemata used to interpret words are context-sensitive as well as culturally sensitive.
claims.source_passage: It is clear that Rosch’s work must be further generalized so that it reflects the context sensitivity as well as the ethnic, cultural sensitivity of our initial schemata.

claims.text: The human mind possesses a remarkable ability to unconsciously adjust initial conceptions of words based on the specific situational context provided by a sentence.
claims.source_passage: The research with the word list just referred to has shown that our minds have a remarkable ability to change initial conception according to the situations in which we find ourselves. Such adjustments are largely unconscious

claims.text: Interpretation is flexible, allowing readers to adjust initial ethnic schemata to specific contextual clues within a text until a good fit is achieved.
claims.source_passage: We apply past schemata to make sense of the incoming words, but these words and other contextual clues affect our initial choices of schemata and our continuing adjustment of them.

claims.text: Efficient reading functions only when the reader has quick access to appropriate schemata; otherwise, the process must be painfully restarted.
claims.source_passage: This process can work efficiently only if the reader has quick access to appropriate schemata. When the appropriate schemata are not quickly available... the process has to be painfully restarted.

claims.text: Psychologists identify at least two radically different types of schemata: those analogous to static pictures and those analogous to scripts or procedures.
claims.source_passage: The latest view (that of Johnson Laird, for instance) is that we use at least two radically different types of schemata, one analogous to static pictures and another to scripts or procedures.

claims.text: Practical advances in teaching literacy do not require a perfect or detailed scientific model of the internal mechanics of schemata.
claims.source_passage: But we do not have to know the fine detail of the process to make intelligent practical advances in the teaching of literacy.

claims.text: Schemata perform two essential functions for literacy: storing knowledge in retrievable form and organizing it for rapid, efficient application.
claims.source_passage: We know that schemata perform two essential functions that are relevant to literacy. The first is storing knowledge in retrievable form; the second is organizing knowledge in more and more efficient ways

claims.text: Without appropriate background knowledge, individuals are unable to adequately understand written or spoken language.
claims.source_passage: Without appropriate background knowledge, people cannot adequately understand written or spoken language.

claims.text: Meaningful assimilation of complex text is impossible below a certain reading speed because the limits of short-term memory cannot integrate un-chunked material.
claims.source_passage: Slowness of reading beyond a certain point makes assimilation of complex meaning impossible. The limits of short-term memory do not allow the integration of "un-chunked" material

claims.text: In the cognitive process of reading, speed of comprehension is functionally equivalent to quality of comprehension.
claims.source_passage: In this process, speed of comprehension is equivalent to quality of comprehension, because without the speed and the shorthand provided by well-organized schemata, our circuits get overloaded.

claims.text: A well-developed schema acts as a mental shorthand, allowing a single surface element to represent a complex underlying structure for rapid manipulation.
claims.source_passage: The evidence has shown that schemata are useful as a kind of mental shorthand that helps us work within the limits of short-term memory. One element of a schema represents many other subterranean elements

claims.text: A well-developed schema allows a person to deploy a complex complex of elements as though it were a single item.
claims.source_passage: In other words, a well-developed schema not only helps us make sense of incoming data but also helps us to manipulate and connect information rapidly.

claims.text: The knowledge most often needed for literacy is located at the 'surface' or top portion of a schema.
claims.source_passage: Their theory is that the knowledge most often needed is also the most directly available and is, so to speak, right at the surface of the schema.

claims.text: Secondary information about a subject is not functional for reading, writing, or conversing.
claims.source_passage: But this secondary information about canaries is not important in communicating with fellow human beings. What is functional in reading, writing, and conversing is the distinctive system of traits in the schemata.

claims.text: Successful communication depends upon the possession of shared cultural associations.
claims.source_passage: Successful communication depends upon shared associations. To participate in the literate national culture is to have acquired a sense of the information that is shared in that culture.

claims.text: Primary associations must be called up in milliseconds to make reading meaningful.
claims.source_passage: When we encounter U. S. Grant, the primary associations must be available to us in milliseconds: that he was an important Union general, that he became president, that he drank.

claims.text: Ordinary adult discourse relies on unstated 'rudiments' of knowledge to maintain communicative efficiency.
claims.source_passage: If assumptions about rudiments could not be made, ordinary discourse would be so lengthy and intricate as to obscure its own point.

claims.text: Literacy requires both intensive knowledge of relationships and extensive knowledge of specifics.
claims.source_passage: But literacy requires us to have both intensive knowledge of relationships and extensive knowledge of specifics.

claims.text: The ability to look up facts is an inadequate substitute for having specific, quickly available schemata already in memory.
claims.source_passage: It is not enough to say that students can look these facts up. The research reviewed above shows that in order for readers to integrate phrases into comprehensible meanings, they must already possess specific, quickly available schemata.

claims.text: Dictionaries and encyclopedias become impractical tools when a reader consistently lacks crucial background information.
claims.source_passage: When readers constantly lack crucial information, dictionaries and encyclopedias become quite impractical tools.

claims.text: All cognitive skills, including reading, are inherently 'knowledge-bound.'
claims.source_passage: Researchers in cognitive psychology and the area of computer science known as artificial intelligence (AI) have come to strikingly similar conclusions about the knowledge-bound character of all cognitive skills.

claims.text: It is more accurate to speak of 'reading skills' in the plural than of a singular, unified 'reading skill.'
claims.source_passage: It is more accurate to speak of “reading skills” than of “reading skill.”

claims.text: A consistent lack of necessary information can make the reading process so laborious and uncommunicative that it fails to convey meaning.
claims.source_passage: A consistent lack of necessary information can make the reading process so laborious and uncommunicative that it fails to convey meaning.

claims.text: Cognitive psychology and artificial intelligence research have reached similar conclusions regarding the knowledge-bound character of all cognitive skills.
claims.source_passage: Researchers in cognitive psychology and the area of computer science known as artificial intelligence (AI) have come to strikingly similar conclusions about the knowledge-bound character of all cognitive skills.

claims.text: The ability to exercise a skill depends on the possession of specific schemata that are sufficiently numerous and detailed to handle the task.
claims.source_passage: AI research demonstrates that the ability of humans to exercise a skill depends on their possession of specific schemata that are sufficiently numerous and detailed to handle the many varieties of the tasks they are called on to perform.

claims.text: A skill is not a unified system of intellectual muscles that can be developed by calisthenics into a vigorous all-purpose ability.
claims.source_passage: a skill is not a unified system of intellectual muscles that can be developed by calisthenics into a vigorous all-purpose ability.

claims.text: The belief that schools can teach reading, writing, and critical thinking as all-purpose general skills applicable to novel problems is highly skeptical.
claims.source_passage: The discoveries that he and his coworkers made should induce a deep skepticism toward the belief that our schools can teach reading, writing, and critical thinking as all-purpose general skills applicable to novel problems.

claims.text: All cognitive skills depend on procedural and substantive schemata that are highly specific to the task at hand.
claims.source_passage: All cognitive skills depend on procedural and substantive schemata that are highly specific to the task at hand.

claims.text: Once relevant knowledge has been acquired, the skill follows.
claims.source_passage: Once the relevant knowledge has been acquired, the skill follows.

claims.text: General programs contrived to teach general skills are ineffective.
claims.source_passage: General programs contrived to teach general skills are ineffective.

claims.text: Experts perform better than novices because they have more relevant and quickly available information, not better intellectual machinery.
claims.source_passage: AI research shows that experts perform better than novices, not because they have more powerful and better-oiled intellectual machinery, but because they have more relevant and quickly available information.

claims.text: Good readers are distinguished from poor ones simply by the possession of a lot of diverse, task-specific information.
claims.source_passage: What distinguishes good readers from poor ones is simply the possession of a lot of diverse, task-specific information.

claims.text: When the configuration of a task is significantly changed, past skills are not transferred to the new problem.
claims.source_passage: When the configuration of a task is significantly changed, past skills are not transferred to the new problem.

claims.text: Artificial intelligence results should be taken seriously because the models actually work in practice (e.g., in chess programs).
claims.source_passage: There is another reason why the AI research results should be taken seriously. AI models actually work. You can play chess against a computer program and lose.

claims.text: Expert performance depends on the quick deployment of schemata.
claims.source_passage: Both approaches show that expert performance depends on the quick deployment of schemata.

claims.text: The convergence of accounts from artificial intelligence (AI) and empirical psychology increases the confidence in the correctness of their models of cognitive processes.
claims.source_passage: The convergence of the accounts from both AI and empirical psychology increases one’s confidence in their correctness.

claims.text: Modern AI models, such as chess programs, succeed because they imitate the knowledge-based procedures of human experts rather than zipping through every possible variation.
claims.source_passage: An up-to-date chess program behaves like a skilled player, not because the computer is directed to zip through every possible variation; it could not possibly do that in the few seconds it takes to respond–but because in some ways, though not in all, it imitates the knowledge-based procedures of experts.

claims.text: Expert chess players possess no better general memories or general skills than novices.
claims.source_passage: In his second experiment de Groot proved that expert chess players have no better memories or general skills than novices. Like everyone else, they can remember only five or six pieces from a random pattern.

claims.text: Experts facilitate cognitive tasks by matching observed patterns with specific schemata already stored in memory.
claims.source_passage: But when they are asked to survey a position that is not random, they quickly match the observed patterns with specific schemata already stored in memory. That process instantly facilitates the cognitive task.

claims.text: The use of positional schemata allows experts to remember complex configurations as single 'chunked' items, staying within the range of short-term memory.
claims.source_passage: Because they already possess positional schemata, experts would need to remember only four or five chunked items well within the range of short-term memory.

claims.text: Skill in reading is fundamentally similar to skill in chess because both require the rapid deployment of pre-acquired schemata.
claims.source_passage: Skill in reading is like skill in chess in many respects. Good reading, like good chess, requires the rapid deployment of schemata that have already been acquired and do not have to be worked out on the spot.

claims.text: Unskilled readers fail because they lack a large store of relevant schemata and must expend cognitive energy working out small-scale meaning relationships.
claims.source_passage: By contrast, unskilled readers lack this large store of relevant schemata and must therefore work out many small-scale meaning relationships while they are reading. These demanding tasks quickly overload their short-term memories, making their performance slow, arduous, and ineffective.

claims.text: The upper limit of items to which a human can have rapid access in a domain is approximately 50,000.
claims.source_passage: Apparently, a number in the range of 50,000 marks the upper limit of items to which we can have rapid access in any domain of activity.

claims.text: A basic vocabulary of 50,000 schemata serves as an index to a much larger volume of knowledge stored in long-term memory.
claims.source_passage: A basic vocabulary of 50,000 schemata serves merely as a quickly accessible index to a much larger volume of knowledge.

claims.text: For mental efficiency in reading or chess, it is most effective to maintain indexed schemata at a medium level of generality.
claims.source_passage: the most effective system for the mind, whether in chess or in reading, is to keep most of its indexed schemata at a medium level of generality.

claims.text: The human mind can store an enormous volume of knowledge by relating any of its 50,000 schemata to others in long-term memory.
claims.source_passage: Any of the 50,000 schemata can be related to others, and the further relationships can be stored in long-term memory.

claims.text: Schemata serve as a mental shorthand where a single symbol or term can represent vast complexes of associated information.
claims.source_passage: A useful illustration of the way one schema can serve as a mental shorthand for enormous complexes of associated schemata is the field of mathematics

claims.text: Verbal expertise is structurally different from chess expertise because verbal schemata must be shared with the community to enable communication.
claims.source_passage: But when we use verbal schemata, we have less freedom. The patterns of association in the verbal schemata of one person must approximate those of another, otherwise we could not use surface meanings to represent larger systems of subsurface associations.

claims.text: Words, idioms, and grammatical systems constitute the shared systems of association that define a common ethnicity.
claims.source_passage: Words, idioms, and grammatical systems represent shared systems of association102 – common to an ethnicity.

claims.text: Young children fail at communication tasks because their messages depend on private associations rather than those widely shared in the speech community.
claims.source_passage: Moreover, the messages depend upon associations that are not widely shared in the speech community.

claims.text: Even ninth graders fail to achieve the level of communicative accuracy that adults display on the first trial of a novel task.
claims.source_passage: We were again surprised at the generally low level of performance of ninth graders, although they showed dramatic improvement in successive trials, still did not attain the virtually perfect accuracy that adults displayed from the very first trial.

claims.text: Children perform poorly on communication tasks not because of insufficient cognitive development, but because they lack information about what others know.
claims.source_passage: Krauss and Glucksberg inferred from this that the older children performed poorly not because of their insufficient cognitive development, but because of their lack of information and insight into what other people might know.

claims.text: Heavy cognitive demands can prevent children from utilizing the social communication skills they actually possess.
claims.source_passage: Finally, when the demands of the task become heavy enough, children may not have the opportunity to bring into play the social communication skills they possess.

claims.text: The performance of poor readers is functionally identical to the performance of children describing unfamiliar shapes: both suffer from a lack of information and resulting cognitive overload.
claims.source_passage: The performance of the young children in this experiment is like the performance of poor readers. Both are ineffective because of lack of information and cognitive overload.

claims.text: A lack of shared information in a speech community strains a person's cognitive capacities during communication tasks.
claims.source_passage: Their lack of information strained their cognitive capacities when they tried to carry out the task.

claims.text: Acquiring literacy is equivalent to acquiring a 'national ethnicity' or a shared communicative store.
claims.source_passage: They had not fully acquired American ethnicity.

claims.text: Children engage in successful social, nonegocentric speech only when the cognitive demands of a task are light.
claims.source_passage: When the demands of the task are relatively light, children do engage in social, nonegocentric speech, and they communicate rather successfully.

claims.text: Effective reading requires the reader to monitor whether their personal associations are shared by the wider speech community.
claims.source_passage: In effective reading, one must not only call up one’s own schematic associations, but also monitor whether they are appropriate ones shared by the wider speech community.

claims.text: Literate adults have internalized shared cultural schemata to the point that they function as second nature.
claims.source_passage: Literate adults have internalized these shared schemata and have made them second nature.

claims.text: Literate adults possess a superior intuition regarding what information is and is not shared within a wider culture compared to children and semi-literates.
claims.source_passage: literate adults have a surer instinct for what will and will not be shared by others in the wider culture. Young children and other semi-literates do not confidently know what other members of the speech community can be expected to know.

claims.text: Beyond a lack of facts, semi-literates specifically lack meta-information about what knowledge is commonly shared by others.
claims.source_passage: young children and semi-literates also lack something equally important: readily accessible information about what is shared by others.

claims.text: Knowledge of a person's unspoken systems of association is essential for successful reading and writing.
claims.source_passage: In reading and writing... it is essential to know the other person’s unspoken systems of association.

claims.text: Ethnic knowledge is not strictly a function of biological age; it depends on acquisition of shared culture.
claims.source_passage: Such ethnic knowledge is not entirely a function of age. Some children acquire it rather early, and illiterate adults never acquire it fully.

claims.text: Children's communication performance correlates strongly with their degree of literacy and social class.
claims.source_passage: performances of children... vary according to the children’s degree of literacy, and their performances therefore have, alas, under present circumstances, a strong correlation with social class.

claims.text: The critical variable for developing a sense of the wider speech community is experience talking with literate strangers.
claims.source_passage: the most important variable was whether the children were used to talking with literate strangers and had thereby gained wide knowledge and a sense of the wider speech community.

claims.text: Literacy is fundamentally a function of a national culture rather than a local culture.
claims.source_passage: Bernstein’s experiments reconfirmed that literacy is a function of a national rather than a local culture.

claims.text: The traditional orderly model of reading (word-to-sentence-to-text meaning) is not wrong in all respects but is so oversimplified that it presents a misleading picture of comprehension.
claims.source_passage: This model isn’t wrong in all respects, but we know that it is so oversimplified and incomplete that it presents a highly misleading picture of the way we understand texts.

claims.text: The constraints of short-term memory (the 4 to 7 item limit) apply universally to every domain of experience, including sound, temperature, and visual points, not just language.
claims.source_passage: Miller’s observation holds true in every domain of experience, not just those presented in language.

claims.text: Memory experts use indirect tricks to circumvent short-term memory limits rather than directly overcoming or expanding the hardwired capacity of the system.
claims.source_passage: Memory experts have worked out indirect tricks to circumvent this universal limitation, but they can never directly overcome it, because the constraint is hardwired into our systems.

claims.text: Chunking is a collaborative process between short-term memory's structuring function and the retrieval of patterns from long-term memory.
claims.source_passage: How do I manage this? Short-term memory holds in momentary suspension items... thus enabling us to convert them into a stable structure. Then we transfer those structures to long term memory... Because I can chunk words into phrasal units and apply my long-term memory capacities as well.

claims.text: Inferences in memory are driven by semantic necessity; for example, people recall 'open' for 'not closed' because it is logically implied, but they do not recall 'short' for 'not tall' because the implication is not absolute.
claims.source_passage: But although they were fooled by a substitution like open for not closed, they were not fooled by a substitution like cold for not hot, presumably because not hot doesn’t necessarily imply cold. Similarly, when the original sentence was 'The man was not tall,' subjects did not assume they had heard 'The man was short,'

claims.text: The provision of context (such as a title or picture) is only effective for constructing a mental model if it is provided BEFORE the reading process begins.
claims.source_passage: Only the group who had been given the title before they started to read could recognize sentences from the passage. The title enabled them to integrate the sentences into a mental model...

claims.text: The human tendency to use middle-level categories (basic-level terms) is a functional strategy for efficiency, as these categories are the most useful instruments for perceiving and acting in the world.
claims.source_passage: We normally interpret experience through the categories that have been most useful to us in the past. We try them out initially as the most efficient instruments for perceiving the world and acting in it.

claims.text: Functional reading requires knowing the 'distinctive system of traits' within a schema that differentiates one object from others in the same class.
claims.source_passage: What is functional in reading, writing, and conversing is the distinctive system of traits in the schemata. We use the traits that differentiate canaries from other birds: their smallness, yellowness, ability to sing...

claims.text: The limit of approximately 50,000 schemata exists because search and retrieval times would become too slow to support rapid comprehension if the indexed items exceeded that number.
claims.source_passage: There must be an upper limit to the number of schemata that we can effectively utilize, because we must have immediate access to them if they are to be effective. ... Using a significantly greater number of items would cause the process of search and retrieval to occupy too much time.

claims.text: Mathematical symbols provide a supreme example of mental shorthand where decades of intellectual labor are reduced to a single schema that can be manipulated as a simple digit.
claims.source_passage: A useful illustration of the way one schema can serve as a mental shorthand... is the field of mathematics, where many decades of mathematical labor can be reduced to a single symbol that can be manipulated as though it were a mere digit like the number five.

evidence.description: An experiment showing that children with low reading ability but high baseball knowledge outperformed 'good' readers with low baseball knowledge when reading a baseball-themed passage.
evidence.source_reference: Recht, Donna R.; Leslie, Lauren, 'Effect of Prior Knowledge on Good and Poor Readers’ Memory of Text', Journal of Educational Psychology, 1988
evidence.source_passage: The children who knew a lot about baseball, but were technically poor readers, read the baseball passage a lot better than the good readers who happened not to know very much about baseball.

evidence.description: Research establishing the severe limits of short-term memory (the 'Magical Number Seven').
evidence.source_reference: G. A. Miller, 'The Magical Number Seven, Plus or Minus Two,' Psychological Review, 1956
evidence.source_passage: The mind cannot reliably hold more than about four to seven separate items in short term memory, whether numbers, letters, points on the body, degrees of loudness, degrees of warmth, and so on.

evidence.description: Foreword to a national report stating that decades of research have produced an unparalleled understanding of language comprehension processes.
evidence.source_reference: Robert Glaser, 'Foreword,' in Becoming a Nation of Readers, 1985
evidence.source_passage: Dr. Robert Glaser... wrote in the foreword to a national report on reading that decades of scientific research “have produced an array of information which is unparalleled in its understanding of the underlying processes in the comprehension of language.”

evidence.description: The observation that people can remember a sequence of numbers (like a phone number) much more easily when they are grouped into smaller units.
Psycholinguistic theory regarding the three-step process of clausal perception and recoding.
evidence.source_reference: T. G. Bever, “Perception and Thought in Language,” 1972

evidence.description: Experiment using target sentences (e.g., about Galileo) to show that subjects could identify meaning changes but could not distinguish between different grammatical forms of the same meaning after a short delay.
evidence.source_reference: Jacqueline Sachs, “Recognition Memory for Syntactic and Semantic Aspects of Connected Discourse,” 1967

evidence.description: A demonstration where subjects recognized changes in meaning (active vs passive voice that changed who sent a letter) but could not distinguish between different grammatical versions of the same meaning.
evidence.source_reference: Jacqueline Sachs (1967)

evidence.description: Recognition tests where subjects frequently selected 'The window is open' as the sentence they heard when the original was actually 'The window is not closed,' but did not make similar mistakes when the synonym wasn't logically equivalent.
evidence.source_reference: S. Fillenbaum (1966)

evidence.description: The 'turtles on a log' experiment showing that subjects 'remembered' a fish swimming under a log (an unstated implication) because their background knowledge of logs and floating made that inference automatic.
evidence.source_reference: J. R. Barclay, J. D. Bransford, and J. J. Franks (1972)

evidence.description: The Bransford, Barclay, and Franks study involving sentences about turtles and logs, which demonstrated that subjects incorporate spatial inferences into their memory of a sentence.
evidence.source_reference: Bransford, J. D., Barclay, J. R., and Franks, J. J. (1972)

evidence.description: Sachs's demonstration showing that people have poor memory for specific words but good memory for the meaning (gist) of sentences.
evidence.source_reference: Sachs, J. S. (1967)

evidence.description: The 'Washing Clothes' experiment where a vague passage was only understood and remembered if the title was provided beforehand, activating prior knowledge.
evidence.source_reference: Bransford, J. D. and Johnson, M. K. (1972)

evidence.description: Trabasso's research comparing five-year-olds and eight-year-olds, attributing reading differences to knowledge acquisition rather than physiological or cognitive development.
evidence.source_reference: Omanson, R. C., Warren, W. H., and Trabasso, T. (1978)

evidence.description: Pearson's 'spider' study where second graders with high topic knowledge outperformed peers with low knowledge but equal general reading ability.
evidence.source_reference: Pearson, P. D., Hanson, J., Gordon, C. (1979)

evidence.description: A study comparing five-year-olds and eight-year-olds, finding knowledge levels were the primary differentiator in reading ability, not memory or reasoning.
evidence.source_reference: Omanson, Warren, and Trabasso, 'Goals, Inferential Comprehension, and Recall of Stories by Children' (1978)

evidence.description: Experiment testing second graders' knowledge of spiders and finding that prior knowledge of the topic significantly improved reading comprehension even when the text lacked difficult vocabulary.
evidence.source_reference: Pearson, Hanson, Gordon, 'The Effect of Background Knowledge on Young Children’s Comprehension' (1979)

evidence.description: Categorization studies where subjects ranked names within categories (fruit, furniture, bird) showing high consistency in typicality (e.g., apple is more typical than olive).
evidence.source_reference: Rosch, 'Cognitive Representation of Semantic Categories' (1975)

evidence.description: Sentence substitution experiment where replacing generic terms with 'typical' members (robin) made more sense to subjects than replacing them with 'atypical' members (ostrich).
Rosch's experimental ranking of furniture, fruit, and birds based on typicality.
evidence.source_reference: E. Rosch, 'Cognitive Representation of Semantic Categories' (1975)
evidence.source_passage: Under “furniture” Rosch found the following ranking: chair, lamp, dresser, cupboard, davenport, radio, footstool, ashtray

evidence.description: Substitution experiment where subjects judged if replacing 'bird' with 'robin' vs 'ostrich' or 'chicken' made sense in context.
evidence.source_passage: if you replace bird with robin in each of the above sentences, subjects always say that the sentences make sense, but if you replace bird with ostrich or chicken, subjects say that the sentences don’t make sense.

evidence.description: Reaction time verification tasks for categorical statements (e.g., 'A robin is a bird').
evidence.source_reference: Rosch (1973)
evidence.source_passage: For instance, it takes a subject much longer to say true or false to the sentence 'A chicken is a bird' than to 'A robin is a bird.'

evidence.description: Comparison of American (robin) vs. Australian (parakeet/galah) typical birds to demonstrate cultural variation in schemata.
evidence.source_reference: Authorial analysis based on Rosch's framework
evidence.source_passage: The creature nearest the Australian schema would be large and colorful, like a parakeet or a galah... It would be odd indeed if the basis for an Australian bird schema were as drab a specimen as a robin.

evidence.description: Analysis of the word 'ball' in different contexts (punter, baby, golfer) to show situational instantiation of meaning.
evidence.source_reference: R. C. Anderson and Z. Shifrin (1980)
evidence.source_passage: A different sort of ball is implied by each sentence. The punter is kicking a football, and the golfer a golf ball... a ball a baby is likely to kick will be hypothecated as perhaps a brightly colored, inflated, plastic ball.

evidence.description: Anderson's sentences (punter, baby, golfer) demonstrating that the words 'kick' and 'ball' are interpreted differently based on the subject's schema.
The Thanksgiving feast example showing that the schema for 'bird' shifts from 'robin' to 'turkey' based on contextual cues.
Research by Collins and Quillian on retrieval time from semantic memory.
evidence.source_reference: A. M. Collins and M. R. Quillian, "Retrieval Time from Semantic Memory," 1969

evidence.description: Theory regarding retrieval time from semantic memory and the surface availability of frequently used knowledge.
evidence.source_reference: A. M. Collins and M. R. Quillian, 'Retrieval Time from Semantic Memory', 1969

evidence.description: Artificial Intelligence research showing that human skill performance depends on possessing numerous, detailed, specific schemata.
evidence.source_reference: Herbert Simon et al., as cited in Endnote 96

evidence.description: Data showing students have high reading skill in familiar domains (friendship) but low skill in unfamiliar domains (Grant and Lee).
evidence.source_reference: Previously cited community college study in Chapter 7

evidence.description: Adriaan de Groot's chess experiments showing grand masters remember 100% of real game positions but perform like novices when pieces are placed randomly.
evidence.source_reference: Adriaan de Groot, Het Denken van den Schaker (1946)
evidence.source_passage: In one experiment, de Groot displayed for five to ten seconds a chess position from an actual game in which twenty-five pieces were left on the board... Grand masters performed this feat with 100 percent accuracy... When the pieces were placed on the board randomly... all his subjects... performed the same as novices did.

evidence.description: Replication of task-specific memory and skill results in the fields of algebra, physics, and medicine.
evidence.source_reference: Chase and Simon (1973); Larkin (1977)
evidence.source_passage: This experiment has been duplicated in several different laboratories, and structurally in several other fields, including algebra, physics, and medicine, always with the same striking results.

evidence.description: Up-to-date chess programs perform like skilled players by imitating knowledge-based procedures rather than calculating every possible variation.
evidence.source_reference: Simon and Barenfield (1969)
evidence.source_passage: An up-to-date chess program behaves like a skilled player... because in some ways, though not in all, it imitates the knowledge-based procedures of experts.

evidence.description: Community college students' disparate reading performance on essays about friendship versus historical figures Grant and Lee.
evidence.source_reference: Not explicitly named in this section, cited as 'The graphs showing...'
evidence.source_passage: The graphs showing the community college students’ high degree of skill in reading the essay on friendship but their lack of skill in reading about Grant and Lee accord with the recent discovery of AI and cognitive psychology

evidence.description: De Groot's experiment showing experts and novices perform equally poorly at remembering random chess positions.
evidence.source_reference: A. de Groot, Thought and Choice in Chess (1965)
evidence.source_passage: In his second experiment de Groot proved that expert chess players have no better memories or general skills than novices. Like everyone else, they can remember only five or six pieces from a random pattern.

evidence.description: Statistical estimation that a chess master can recognize 50,000 positional patterns.
evidence.source_reference: Larkin, et al., ‘Expert and Novice Performance in Solving Physics Problems’
evidence.source_passage: It has been estimated that a chess master can recognize about 50,000 positional patterns.

evidence.description: The typical vocabulary size of a literate person is approximately 50,000 words and idioms.
evidence.source_reference: Simon and Barenfeld (1969)
evidence.source_passage: Interestingly, that is the approximate number of words and idioms in the vocabulary of a literate person.

evidence.description: The observation that modern chess programs succeed by imitating knowledge-based procedures rather than brute-force calculation.
evidence.source_reference: Chase and Simon, ‘Perception in Chess’ (1973)
evidence.source_passage: AI models actually work. You can play chess against a computer program and lose... because in some ways, though not in all, it imitates the knowledge-based procedures of experts.

evidence.description: Mathematical symbols used as a representation of decades of labor reduced to a single digit-like symbol.
Communication experiment involving two subjects separated by a barrier who must describe unfamiliar shapes to one another.
evidence.source_reference: R. M. Krauss and S. Glucksberg, 'Social and Nonsocial Speech,' 1977

evidence.description: Chart showing idiosyncratic descriptions by children for novel forms, such as 'Daddy's shirt' or 'Mother's hat,' which failed to communicate the shape to a listener.
Longitudinal testing of children from kindergarten through ninth grade in the Princeton school system on communication tasks.
evidence.source_reference: Krauss and Glucksberg (1977)

evidence.description: Experiments where children and adults had to describe strange shapes across a physical barrier to evaluate social vs. nonsocial speech.
evidence.source_reference: Krauss, R. M. & Glucksberg, S., 'Social and Nonsocial Speech,' Scientific American, 1977
evidence.source_passage: As the Krauss-Glucksberg experiments indicate, literate adults have a surer instinct for what will and will not be shared by others in the wider culture.

evidence.description: Experiments showing that communication task performance varies by literacy level and correlates with social class based on exposure to 'literate strangers.'
evidence.source_reference: Bernstein, B., 'Social Class, Language and Socialization,' 1972
evidence.source_passage: In Bernstein’s experiments, the most important variable was whether the children were used to talking with literate strangers and had thereby gained wide knowledge and a sense of the wider speech community.

concepts.author_definition: A 'delusion' that schools teach transferable skills like 'finding the main idea' that supposedly function independently of specific background knowledge.
concepts.common_alternative: The standard pedagogical view that reading is a set of transferable cognitive skills.
concepts.source_passage: Current science explains why the claim made by our schools that they are teaching general “reading-comprehension skills” is a delusion.

concepts.author_definition: A lack of the national-ethnic background knowledge shared by the 'good readers' in a society, assuming decoding skills are present.
concepts.common_alternative: A lack of innate cognitive ability or transferable linguistic skills.
concepts.source_passage: It simply means low national-ethnic background knowledge shared by the good readers in a society.

concepts.author_definition: Grouping discrete items into smaller numbers of groupings to circumvent short-term memory limits.
concepts.source_passage: One device that memory experts have developed to resist this built-in limitation is “chunking.”

concepts.author_definition: The theory that explicit meanings of a text are only a small portion of its total meaning, most of which relies on the reader's unstated knowledge.
concepts.source_passage: The explicit meanings of a piece of writing are the tip of an iceberg of meaning; the larger part lies below the surface of the text and is composed of the reader’s own relevant knowledge

concepts.author_definition: A temporary cognitive 'vestibule' that holds incoming temporal language items just long enough to structure them into nontemporal units.
The stable, abstract meaning of a sentence or clause that is transferred to long-term memory while the specific literal words are discarded.
The literal, precise wording and syntactic structure of a sentence, which is rapidly lost after the initial processing.
The theory that language understanding is an active process of building a mental model of what words imply by using background knowledge, rather than a passive recording of literal word meanings.
concepts.common_alternative: The theory that we interpret and store the literal meanings of clauses and sentences in long-term memory.

concepts.author_definition: An integrated cognitive structure formed from both text information and prior knowledge that allows a reader to understand and store discourse.
Classifications that are neither overly specific (like 'oak') nor overly general (like 'flora'), serving as the 'basic furniture' of the conceptual world and the first terms children learn.
concepts.common_alternative: General taxonomies used in scientific or formal logic.
concepts.source_passage: Eleanor Rosch has called these middle categories basic-level terms... middle-level categories are the basic furniture of our conceptual world.

concepts.author_definition: The cultural tendency to associate a category with a specific, commonly encountered example (e.g., North Americans associating 'tree' with leaves rather than fronds).
The cognitive constraint imposed by the limits of short-term memory, requiring past knowledge to give meaning to sensory fragments.
A mental model or specific example of a category that people within a culture hold 'at the ready' and use to process language and perception rapidly.
concepts.common_alternative: A dictionary definition or set of logical criteria for category membership.
concepts.source_passage: For a bird in American culture, the prototype is a vaguely robin-like creature.

concepts.author_definition: Abstract mental entities, models, or frames used to organize past knowledge and assimilate new information during comprehension.
The process of making present experiences take on meaning by fitting them into existing mental prototypes or structures.
concepts.common_alternative: The broader Piagetian term that includes both fitting data to mind and mind to data.
concepts.source_passage: We are able to make our present experiences take on meaning by assimilating them to prototypes formed from our past experiences.

concepts.author_definition: A cognitive structure where one surface element represents a complex set of subterranean elements, allowing for rapid information processing.
Information that has not been organized into efficient schemata, making it difficult to process within the limits of short-term memory.
The distinctive system of traits and facts commonly associated with a term within a specific culture that allow for rapid mental deployment.
The principle that the exercise of a cognitive skill is inseparable from and dependent upon the possession of domain-specific information.
A broad breadth of specific factual information, as opposed to 'intensive knowledge' which focuses on a few cases in depth.
Groups of individual pieces of information (like letters or chess pieces) that are perceived as a single meaningful unit, thereby reducing the burden on short-term memory.
An optimal level of schema categorization that is specific enough to be useful but general enough to represent multiple associated variations of knowledge.
The shared system of verbal schemata, words, idioms, and associations common to the language community that enables effective communication with strangers.
A state where the mind is forced to figure out too many things at once due to a lack of pre-existing shared information required for a task.
Internalized mental structures of association that are common to the wider speech community and enable rapid comprehension without exhaustive explanation.
The distinction between the shared communicative store of the entire nation (necessary for literacy) versus the specialized or limited knowledge used within a specific neighborhood or family.
concepts.source_passage: Bernstein’s experiments reconfirmed that literacy is a function of a national rather than a local culture.

concepts.author_definition: Hilary Putnam's term for the mental models or prototypes that individuals use to make sense of present experiences by assimilating them to past knowledge.
concepts.source_passage: Knowing about prototypes is essential for understanding how the people within a given ethnicity apply past knowledge to the comprehension of speech. These mental models are the same as Hilary Putnam’s 'stereotypes.'

concepts.author_definition: The specific set of primary characteristics (e.g., yellowness for a canary) that differentiate one member of a category from others and are essential for functional communication.
concepts.source_passage: What is functional in reading, writing, and conversing is the distinctive system of traits in the schemata.

concepts.author_definition: A metaphorical description of short-term memory where incoming items are held briefly in equality to allow the mind to assign them structure.
concepts.source_passage: Short-term memory is the mind’s vestibule where incoming items enjoy a brief equality lasting just long enough for the mind to give them a structure.

cases.name: The Baseball Experiment
cases.description: A study conducted in the 1980s by Recht and Leslie involving junior high students. The researchers compared reading comprehension between 'good' and 'poor' readers (as defined by standard tests) based on their knowledge of baseball. The study found that students who were poor readers but had high baseball knowledge recalled and summarized the text as well as the 'good' readers with high knowledge, and far better than 'good' readers with low knowledge.
key_passages: The children who knew a lot about baseball, but were technically poor readers, read the baseball passage a lot better than the good readers who happened not to know very much about baseball.
Students with high reading ability and high knowledge did not recall better or summarize better than did students with low reading ability and high knowledge.
contestable_aspects: The definition of 'poor reader' vs 'good reader'
The transferability of the finding to other non-sports domains

cases.name: The Spider Knowledge Experiment
cases.description: Equally talented second graders were tested on their knowledge of spiders before reading a story about them. Those with prior spider knowledge performed significantly better at reading the story, despite the text lacking complex vocabulary or concepts.
key_passages: In one experiment, equally talented second graders were tested to find out how much they knew about spiders. Those who knew a little about them were much better readers of a story involving spiders than were the other children, even though the story contained no special concepts or difficult words.

cases.name: The View from Hirsch's Window
cases.description: Hirsch describes looking at trees and seeing only fragments (leaves and branches), yet cognitively perceiving 'trees' because his past knowledge fills in the unseen trunks and roots.
key_passages: When I ask people what they see out of my window, they invariably answer 'trees.'... Without being able to view a single tree trunk or top or bottom, they nonetheless say that they see trees.

cases.name: American Bird Schema
cases.description: In American culture, the mental prototype for 'bird' is a creature similar to a robin (drab, sits on branches, twitters, flies). This affects reaction time in tests and judgment of sentence validity.
key_passages: For a bird in American culture, the prototype is a vaguely robin-like creature.

cases.name: Australian Bird Schema
cases.description: Hypothetical but culturally grounded comparison where the mental prototype for 'bird' is a large, colorful bird like a parakeet or galah, reflecting the common environment of Australia.
key_passages: The creature nearest the Australian schema would be large and colorful, like a parakeet or a galah, with bright scarlet, green, and blue plumage

cases.name: The Canary Schema
cases.description: The author uses the concept of a canary to illustrate the difference between functional cultural knowledge (small, yellow, sings) and secondary biological/physical facts (has DNA, obeys gravity) that are not used in rapid communication.
key_passages: We use the traits that differentiate canaries from other birds: their smallness, yellowness, ability to sing, use in human culture, being kept in cages, and so on.

cases.name: U.S. Grant Associations
cases.description: The author cites the name 'U.S. Grant' to show that reading comprehension requires immediate retrieval of specific implicit facts (Union general, president, drinker) that are not explicitly stated in text.
key_passages: When we encounter U. S. Grant, the primary associations must be available to us in milliseconds: that he was an important Union general, that he became president, that he drank.

cases.name: De Groot's Chess Players
cases.description: A study conducted by Dutch psychologist Adriaan de Groot in which players of varying expertise were asked to reproduce chess board configurations. When the configurations came from real games, experts were vastly superior. When the configurations were random, the experts' advantage vanished, demonstrating that 'skill' was actually context-specific knowledge memory.
key_passages: De Groot discovered that chess masters are astonishingly skilled at remembering and reproducing chess positions after a very brief exposure to them.
Instead of placing the twenty-five pieces in positions from an actual game, he placed them on the board randomly. The results were unexpected... All his subjects... performed the same as novices did.
contestable_aspects: Whether memory for patterns in chess is perfectly analogous to reading comprehension.

cases.name: De Groot's chess experiments
cases.description: Adriaan de Groot tested chess masters and novices by exposing them to chessboards for six seconds. When positions were random, both groups performed poorly (remembering 5-6 pieces). When positions were from actual games, masters perfectly reconstructed the boards while novices failed, demonstrating that expertise is based on stored patterns rather than superior memory.
key_passages: In his second experiment de Groot proved that expert chess players have no better memories or general skills than novices.
When they are asked to survey a position that is not random, they quickly match the observed patterns with specific schemata already stored in memory.

cases.name: Princeton school system communication study
cases.description: A study where students from kindergarten to ninth grade were tested on their ability to describe unfamiliar shapes to a listener behind a barrier. It found that even the oldest children did not reach adult levels of initial communicative accuracy because they had not yet mastered the shared schemata of the speech community.
key_passages: We proceeded to test children from grades three through nine in the Princeton school system.
They had not fully acquired American ethnicity.
contestable_aspects: Whether 'American ethnicity' is the correct label for the observed communicative gap

cases.name: Krauss-Glucksberg Shape Communication Task
cases.description: An experiment where subjects must describe abstract shapes to another person across a barrier. Young children and semi-literates fail when the task demands increase because they do not know what information is 'shared' with the other person, leading to cognitive overload.
key_passages: A semiliterate person reading or a young child describing strange shapes has to figure out too many things at one time.
Young children and other semi-literates do not confidently know what other members of the speech community can be expected to know.

thinkers.name: Robert Glaser
thinkers.key_idea: Decades of research have created an unparalleled understanding of the comprehension process.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Glaser's authority as president of the National Academy of Education to validate the scientific basis for his claims about reading.
key_passages: In 1985, Dr. Robert Glaser... wrote... that decades of scientific research “have produced an array of information which is unparalleled in its understanding of the underlying processes in the comprehension of language.”

thinkers.name: George Miller
thinkers.key_idea: The human mind has a severely limited short-term memory capacity of approximately seven items.
thinkers.source_work: The Magical Number Seven, Plus or Minus Two (1956)
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch identifies Miller's work as the psychological foundation for understanding why background knowledge (and chunking) is necessary for reading comprehension.
key_passages: Miller noticed, though, that our capacity for remembering briefly presented and disconnected items is severely limited. The mind cannot reliably hold more than about four to seven separate items in short term memory

thinkers.name: T. G. Bever
thinkers.key_idea: The clause is the primary perceptual unit and language is processed by recoding clauses into abstract forms after semantic relations are assigned.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Bever's three-step explanation of language processing to explain how meaning is extracted and stored despite memory limitations.
key_passages: As the psycholinguist T. G. Bever explains in three steps : “(1) The clause is the primary perceptual unit; (2) within each clause we assign semantic relations within major phrases; (3) after each clause is processed, it is recoded into relatively abstract form, thereby leaving short-term memory available for processing the next clause.”

thinkers.name: Jacqueline Sachs
thinkers.key_idea: Recognition memory for the syntactic (surface) form of connected discourse is lost much faster than memory for semantic (meaning) aspects.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Sachs' 1967 study as empirical proof that people rapidly forget the exact words used in a narrative while retaining the meaning.
key_passages: In 1967 Jacqueline Sachs reported a demonstration of this phenomenon. She read her subjects narratives that were one paragraph long. Each paragraph contained a target sentence... Sachs inferred from these results...

thinkers.name: J. D. Bransford
thinkers.key_idea: The constructive nature of language comprehension and the necessity of background knowledge for initial understanding.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch highlights Bransford's work (with Barclay and Franks) as the 'next phase' of research that proved understanding depends on applying unstated knowledge.
key_passages: In a series of experiments, they found that our initial understanding of a text depends on our applying relevant background knowledge that is not given in the text itself.

thinkers.name: R. J. Spiro
thinkers.key_idea: The discovery that inferential elaborations are a standard part of the prose understanding process was supported by a massive amount of research following the Bransford studies.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Spiro to emphasize that the constructive hypothesis of meaning is not an isolated finding but a widely confirmed scientific consensus.
key_passages: According to R. J. Spiro, 'The study by Bransford, Barclay and Franks served as a prototype for what was to become a near avalanche of demonstrations that inferential elaborations are a part of the process of understanding prose.'

thinkers.name: T. Trabasso
thinkers.key_idea: Reading ability differences in young children are due to knowledge levels, not biological maturation or reasoning skill.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Trabasso to rebut the developmentalist idea that reading progress is limited by innate cognitive capacities or 'maturation' stages.
key_passages: T. Trabasso and his colleagues discovered that differences in reading ability between five-year-olds and eight-year-olds are caused primarily by the older children’s possessing more knowledge

thinkers.name: Eleanor Rosch
thinkers.key_idea: The primacy of middle-level (basic) categories in human cognition and the internal structure of semantic categories based on typicality.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Rosch's research to explain why shared background knowledge is structured around specific common examples and how this enables communicative efficiency.
key_passages: Eleanor Rosch has called these middle categories basic-level terms. We owe much of our recent insight into their primacy to work done by her and her associates.

thinkers.name: Hilary Putnam
thinkers.key_idea: The concept of 'stereotypes' in language and meaning.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch equates Putnam's 'stereotypes' with the psychological prototypes used in his argument.
key_passages: These mental models are the same as Hilary Putnam’s 'stereotypes.'

thinkers.name: R. C. Anderson
thinkers.key_idea: Schema theory and the instantiation of general terms based on context.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch adopts Anderson's term 'schema' and uses his 'kick the ball' examples to show context sensitivity in mental models.
key_passages: Researchers who have been relating these mental entities to reading, particularly R. C. Anderson and his associates, have chosen the word schema for them

thinkers.name: Jean Piaget
thinkers.key_idea: The dual processes of assimilation and accommodation in cognitive development.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch accepts Piaget's concepts of assimilation and accommodation while explicitly rejecting his general view of developmentalism.
key_passages: Jean Piaget’s notion of assimilation and its contrast with accommodation, are still useful concepts, despite the disconfirmation of his general view of 'development.'

thinkers.name: P. N. Johnson-Laird
thinkers.key_idea: We use at least two different types of schemata (static pictures and scripts/procedures).
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Johnson-Laird to show that while the internal details of schemata are complex, their existence and functions are well-established.
key_passages: The latest view (that of Johnson Laird, for instance) is that we use at least two radically different types of schemata

thinkers.name: A. M. Collins and M. R. Quillian
thinkers.key_idea: Research on the speed and mechanics of retrieval from semantic memory.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites them to transition into the importance of efficiency and speed in how schemata function.
key_passages: A. M. Collins and M. R. Quillian published an important piece of work on this subject.

thinkers.name: Jean-Jacques Rousseau and John Dewey
thinkers.key_idea: A tradition that favors less emphasis on 'mere fact' and more on intensive study of a few cases to teach critical thinking.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch argues this tradition fails to recognize that literacy requires a broad breadth of specific facts (extensive knowledge) to function.
