key_passages: Educators in the Rousseau-Dewey tradition... encourage us to believe that students will thereby understand general principles and learn how to think critically.

thinkers.name: Herbert A. Simon
thinkers.key_idea: Expert performance is caused by specific knowledge rather than a general 'dormative power' or 'skill.'
thinkers.source_work: Larkin, McDermott, Simon, and Simon, 'Expert and Novice Performance in Solving Physics Problems' (1980)
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Simon's skepticism of general skills to validate his own argument that schools must teach content. He cites Simon's wry comparison to Moliere to debunk the reification of 'skill.'
key_passages: Dr. Herbert A. Simon, a leading figure in AI research, once wryly remarked that saying an expert performance is caused by a “skill” is like Moliere’s doctor saying that the sleep-inducing properties of opium are caused by its “dormative power.”
Simon and his colleagues have cast doubt on the idea that there are any general or transferable cognitive skills.

thinkers.name: Adriaan de Groot
thinkers.key_idea: The thinking of chess players is based on pattern recognition from previous knowledge (schemata) rather than general superior memory.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch presents De Groot's work as the foundational empirical evidence that expertise does not transfer across domains.
key_passages: Probably the most dramatic illustrations of the knowledge-bound character of human skills came from some remarkable experiments conducted by Adriaan de Groot

thinkers.name: R. M. Krauss and S. Glucksberg
thinkers.key_idea: The inability of children to communicate successfully in certain tasks is due to a lack of shared social information rather than developmental stages.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses their 1977 experiment as the primary empirical evidence for the necessity of shared schemata ('ethnicity') in literacy and communication.
key_passages: The work was conducted and reported by R. M. Krauss and S. Glucksberg.103
Krauss and Glucksberg inferred from this that the older children performed poorly not because of their insufficient cognitive development, but because of their lack of information

thinkers.name: Basil Bernstein
thinkers.key_idea: Distinction between local socialization and national socialization in verbal tasks.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch links Bernstein's discoveries about class-based language codes to the concept of national versus local socialization.
key_passages: The contrast between the performances of adults and children parallels the contrasts Basil Bernstein has discovered

actors.name: National Commission on Reading
actors.context: The body that produced the report Glaser prefaced, which updated the model of reading comprehension.

actors.name: Classroom teachers
actors.context: According to Hirsch, they were forced to decide curricula themselves due to a lack of coherent national guidelines, leading to the 'delusion' of general skills.

actors.name: Robert Glaser
actors.context: Cited by Hirsch as having referred to 'new insights about language processing' that connect memory constraints to literacy.

actors.name: S. Fillenbaum
actors.context: Psycholinguist whose 1966 work on synonym substitution and 'memory for gist' supports Hirsch's claim about semantic memory.

actors.name: W.F. Brewer
actors.context: Researcher who demonstrated the robustness of synonym substitution in 1975.

actors.name: J. S. Sachs
actors.context: Researcher who demonstrated that subjects remember the 'gist' but not the specific syntax of sentences.

actors.name: P. D. Pearson
actors.context: Researcher who demonstrated the impact of specific background knowledge on the reading performance of second graders.

actors.name: P. D. Pearson
actors.context: Researcher who demonstrated the link between specific prior knowledge and reading comprehension in seven-year-olds.

actors.name: American Educators
actors.context: The author claims they have ignored the obvious implications of cognitive research regarding background knowledge.

actors.name: Eleanor Rosch
actors.context: Cognitive psychologist who established prototype theory.

actors.name: R. C. Anderson
actors.context: Researcher on reading and schema theory.

actors.name: Psychologists
actors.context: The group of researchers currently attempting to model the fine details of how schemata function in the mind.

actors.name: Semiliterate people
actors.context: The group Hirsch identifies as needing literacy improvements based on the application of schema principles.

actors.name: Educators in the Rousseau-Dewey tradition
actors.context: Educational theorists who prioritize intensive study and general skills over broad factual knowledge.

actors.name: Cognitive Psychologists and AI Researchers
actors.context: Scholars whose research converges on the idea that skills are knowledge-dependent.

actors.name: Herbert A. Simon
actors.context: Nobel laureate and AI researcher who debunked general skills.

actors.name: Adriaan de Groot
actors.context: Dutch psychologist who conducted chess memory experiments.

actors.name: Artificial Intelligence (AI) Scientists
actors.context: Researchers who create computer models of human performance confirming the need for specific schemata.

actors.name: Chess Masters
actors.context: Used as the prototypical example of expertise through schemata.

actors.name: Artificial Intelligence Research
actors.context: Cited as a field that corroborates psychological findings on the importance of knowledge-based procedures.

actors.name: Princeton school system
actors.context: The site of the Krauss and Glucksberg research on children's communicative abilities.

actors.name: Otto Jespersen
actors.context: Cited regarding the evolution of language from cumbersome redundancies to shared implicit conventions.

actors.name: Basil Bernstein
actors.context: Sociolinguist whose work links social class to communication styles and literacy.

actors.name: Robert Krauss
actors.context: Researcher in social and nonsocial speech patterns.

actors.name: Sam Glucksberg
actors.context: Researcher in cognitive psychology and communication.

objections_raised.objection: Schools claim that they are teaching general 'reading-comprehension skills.'
objections_raised.response: These skills do not exist as transferable entities; comprehension is domain-specific and dependent on background knowledge.

objections_raised.objection: How do we remember the meanings of whole conversations and books if short-term memory is so fleeting and limited?
objections_raised.response: We manage this by processing language in clauses, recoding the meaning into a 'gist' or abstract form, and transferring that gist to long-term memory, which frees up short-term memory for new input.

objections_raised.objection: Language comprehension might simply involve interpreting and storing the literal meanings of clauses and sentences in long-term memory.
objections_raised.source: Unnamed psycholinguistic theorists (contrasted with Barclay, Bransford, and Franks)
objections_raised.response: The 'constructive hypothesis' was proven correct by experiments showing that subjects 'remember' unstated implications as if they were explicit words, which is only possible if they are building and storing a fuller mental model based on background knowledge.

objections_raised.objection: The results of background knowledge experiments (like the spider study) might be dismissed as 'predictable and obvious.'
objections_raised.response: Despite being intuitive, these findings are essential for education because they contradict the dominant pedagogical focus on 'general reading skills' over 'knowledge.'

objections_raised.objection: The results showing that background knowledge helps reading comprehension are 'predictable and obvious.'
objections_raised.response: If they are obvious, educators have still failed to acknowledge or act upon their implications in curriculum and instruction.

objections_raised.objection: Psychologists do not yet understand the fine details of how schemata function and are modeled in the mind.
objections_raised.response: Detailed scientific modeling is not required to apply the known general principles of schemata to improve literacy instruction.

objections_raised.objection: Students can simply look up facts they do not know in dictionaries or encyclopedias.
objections_raised.response: Looking up facts is too slow for the reading process; readers need quickly available schemata to integrate meanings in real-time.

objections_raised.objection: Intensive study of a few cases is better because it teaches general principles and critical thinking.
objections_raised.response: Literacy specifically requires 'extensive' knowledge of many specifics; without it, reading becomes too laborious to convey meaning.

objections_raised.objection: Skills like reading and critical thinking are all-purpose intellectual muscles that can be improved through general practice (calisthenics).
objections_raised.response: Research in AI and cognitive psychology shows that experts do not have better 'machinery' (muscles), but rather more relevant and task-specific information (schemata). When tasks change, the skill does not transfer.

objections_raised.objection: Expertise might be the result of superior general memory or general problem-solving skills.
objections_raised.response: De Groot's experiments showed that when patterns were random (removing the knowledge advantage), experts performed no better than novices.

objections_raised.objection: Children perform poorly on complex communication tasks because they have not reached the necessary stage of cognitive development.
objections_raised.response: Krauss and Glucksberg's research suggests the failure is actually due to a lack of specific shared information about what other people know, leading to cognitive overload.
